# PRD: Phase 2a — Google Workspace OAuth Foundation

**Generated by:** Lopmon (CLM Studios)
**Date:** 2026-04-17
**Status:** ✅ Approved by Citlali — ready for coding agent
**Requested by:** Citlali (Phase 2 kickoff)

---

## 1. Goal

Get a real OAuth 2.0 flow working for Google Workspace — a user can click "Connect Google" inside Edify, authorize Calendar + Gmail + Drive scopes, and we store working access + refresh tokens. Verify the loop end-to-end by listing the user's calendars from a simple test endpoint.

This is the foundation for Phase 2b (Calendar tools), 2c (Gmail tools), 2d (Drive tools) — each of which adds the actual API surface for the archetype directors. We don't wire any director to any tool in this PRD.

## 2. Why

Phase 1 unblocked the substrate. Phase 2 unblocks the integrations. Without working OAuth + token storage, no Google-touching feature can exist. The roadmap puts Phase 2 second specifically because it touches the most directors (EA, Events, Marketing, Development) and is the highest "wow factor" — but all of that depends on this OAuth foundation.

The simplify pass earlier tonight made `/api/integrations/callback` return 501 for real OAuth codes (replacing a silent fake-success). This PRD turns that 501 into a working token exchange.

## 3. Scope

### In scope

1. **Install Google SDKs** in `apps/web/`
   - `googleapis` (the official Google API client library — covers Calendar, Gmail, Drive in one package)

2. **OAuth credentials reuse**
   - Use the existing Google OAuth client created 2026-04-16 (creds in `.env.local` as `GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_ID` and `GOOGLE_OAUTH_CLIENT_SECRET`)
   - The existing redirect URIs include `http://localhost:3000/auth/callback` and the Supabase callback. We need to add a NEW redirect URI for integration OAuth (separate from sign-in OAuth):
     - `https://edifyos.vercel.app/api/integrations/google/callback`
     - `http://localhost:3000/api/integrations/google/callback`
   - **Citlali action:** add these two redirect URIs in the Google Cloud Console after the agent finishes (Lopmon will provide the link). Until added, OAuth will fail in production with a redirect_uri_mismatch error.

3. **OAuth initiate route** — `apps/web/src/app/api/integrations/google/connect/route.ts` (NEW)
   - GET — returns a redirect URL to Google's auth page
   - Requests scopes for all 3 services upfront:
     - `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/calendar`
     - `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/gmail.modify` (read + draft + send)
     - `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file` (per-file access — safer than full drive)
     - `https://www.googleapis.com/auth/userinfo.email` (so we know which Google account got connected)
   - Includes `access_type: "offline"` and `prompt: "consent"` to guarantee a refresh token (Google only issues refresh tokens on first consent or with explicit prompt)
   - Includes a CSRF state token (random, stored in a short-lived signed cookie) — verified in callback
   - Auth-gates to logged-in users with an org

4. **OAuth callback route** — `apps/web/src/app/api/integrations/google/callback/route.ts` (NEW)
   - GET — receives `code` + `state` from Google
   - Validates the CSRF state cookie
   - Exchanges code for `{ access_token, refresh_token, expires_in, scope }`
   - Calls Google's userinfo endpoint with the access token to get the connected Google account email
   - Inserts/upserts THREE rows into `integrations` table — one for each of `gmail`, `google_calendar`, `google_drive`:
     - Same access_token + refresh_token (Google issues one set of tokens; the scope determines what they can hit)
     - `scopes` array contains only the scopes relevant to that integration type
     - `config: { google_email: "..." }` so the UI can show "Connected as alice@example.com"
     - `connected_by: memberId`, `status: 'active'`
   - On success, redirect user to `/dashboard/integrations?connected=google` (or wherever the integrations management UI lives — agent should check; if no such page exists, redirect to `/dashboard?google=connected`)

5. **Make the existing `/api/integrations/callback` route a stub-rejector for now**
   - It already returns 501 for real codes after the simplify pass
   - Add a comment explaining that Google has its own dedicated callback at `/api/integrations/google/callback` and other integrations (Slack, Salesforce, etc.) will follow the same per-provider pattern in their own PRDs

6. **Token refresh helper** — `apps/web/src/lib/google.ts` (NEW)
   - Export `getValidGoogleAccessToken(serviceClient, orgId, integrationType)` that:
     - Reads the integration row
     - If `token_expires_at` is in the future (with 60s buffer), returns the existing access token
     - Otherwise, calls Google's token refresh endpoint with the stored refresh token, updates the row with new access_token + new expires_at, returns the new access token
     - Returns `{ accessToken: string } | { error: NextResponse }` similar to the `getAnthropicClientForOrg` pattern
   - Throws/returns a clear error if the integration is missing or the refresh fails (e.g., user revoked access on Google's side)

7. **Test endpoint to verify the loop** — `apps/web/src/app/api/integrations/google/test-calendars/route.ts` (NEW)
   - GET — uses `getValidGoogleAccessToken` to list the user's Calendars via `googleapis` (`calendar.calendarList.list()`)
   - Returns `{ calendars: [{ id, summary, primary }] }` or a clear error
   - This is purely a smoke test — Phase 2b will replace it with the real Calendar tools

8. **UI affordance** — minimal
   - Find the existing integrations list UI (audit before building — likely under `/dashboard/integrations` or similar)
   - Add a "Connect Google" button if Google isn't connected, "Connected as {email}" + "Disconnect" if connected
   - Keep the styling minimal — match whatever pattern the existing integrations list uses
   - "Disconnect" action: DELETE on `/api/integrations/google` (a small DELETE handler that nukes the 3 rows)

### Out of scope (Phase 2b/c/d)

- Calendar event creation/listing beyond the smoke test (Phase 2b)
- Gmail read/draft/send (Phase 2c)
- Drive read/create (Phase 2d)
- Wiring any of these into archetype chat tools (each Phase 2b/c/d's job)
- Token revocation cleanup if a user removes Edify access from their Google account (Phase 2 later)
- Multi-Google-account-per-org support (rare, deferred)
- Webhook/push notifications for calendar/gmail changes (separate PRD)
- Per-user Google connections (Edify is org-scoped — one Google connection per org, all members share it. This matches the roadmap's "your org" framing and keeps tokens centralized.)

## 4. Architecture Decisions

- **Org-scoped, not user-scoped Google connection.** One Google account is connected per Edify org. All members of the org share the connection. Trade-off: simpler model, matches roadmap; downside is the connecting member must have access to whichever Google account the org uses (e.g., a shared `info@nonprofit.org`). For a nonprofit ops platform this is the right fit.
- **All 3 service scopes requested in one OAuth flow.** User authorizes once, gets all 3 capabilities. Simpler than per-service consent flows.
- **One token set, three integration rows.** Google issues one access+refresh token set per OAuth flow. We mirror it across 3 rows in `integrations` so the existing per-service status/scope tracking stays clean. The `getValidGoogleAccessToken` helper updates all 3 rows when refreshing (via a small batch update).
  - Caveat the agent should consider: if updating 3 rows with the same fresh token feels racy, an alternative is to store one row keyed on `(org_id, 'google')` and have a separate "enabled scopes" array. But that breaks the existing schema's per-service rows. For now: 3 rows, batch update on refresh.
- **Tokens stored "encrypted" — but actually plaintext for now.** Same as Anthropic key. Column names are aspirational. Real encryption is a follow-up PRD covering all sensitive columns at once.
- **No queue/background job for token refresh.** Refresh happens on demand inside `getValidGoogleAccessToken`. Acceptable since refresh is fast and only happens hourly per integration. Background refresh is a Phase-later optimization.

## 5. Acceptance Criteria

Done when:
1. ✅ Manually clicking "Connect Google" from the integrations UI redirects to Google → consent screen shows Calendar + Gmail + Drive + email scopes → on accept, redirects back → integrations UI shows "Connected as {email}" for all 3 Google services.
2. ✅ `GET /api/integrations/google/test-calendars` returns a real list of the connected Google account's calendars.
3. ✅ Calling `getValidGoogleAccessToken` with an expired token successfully refreshes and returns a working token.
4. ✅ "Disconnect" button removes all 3 Google integration rows; clicking "Connect Google" again works without errors.
5. ✅ CSRF state mismatch returns a clear 400 error, not a token exchange.
6. ✅ `npm run build` clean.
7. ✅ Single commit on main, message starting `feat: Google Workspace OAuth (Phase 2a)`.

## 6. Risks & Open Questions

- **Google OAuth verification status.** The Google OAuth client is in "Testing" mode (set up 2026-04-16). In Testing mode, only test users on the consent screen's allowlist can authorize. If a real client tries to connect, they'll see "Access blocked: Edify OS has not completed Google verification." For Phase 2a smoke testing this is fine — the agent can list `edifysaas@gmail.com` as the only test user, or use a personal Google account for testing. **Production verification with Google is a separate, weeks-long process** that Z would need to initiate. Flag this clearly to Lopmon at the end.
- **Redirect URI registration.** The Google Cloud Console must list the new callback URLs. If the agent can't do this (no GCP CLI/credentials available), it should write a clear "Citlali action needed" entry in SESSION-LOG with the exact URIs to add and where.
- **`integrations` table RLS.** The simplify pass added INSERT policies for `orgs` and `members`. The `integrations` table also needs INSERT policies for any future direct-from-client use. For Phase 2a, the API routes use the service client (RLS-bypassing), so this isn't blocking — but the agent should add appropriate policies as part of the same migration.
- **Testing with `edifyos.vercel.app` vs localhost.** The agent can build and verify build, but full end-to-end testing requires either localhost or the live deployment. Citlali will need to test the live flow after the agent finishes.

## 7. Estimated Effort

~1–2 coding sessions (Sonnet agent, 1.5–3 hours).

## 8. Approval

- [x] Citlali approved (2026-04-17 ~10:16 PM EDT)
- [x] Architecture pre-locked (org-scoped, all-scopes-upfront, 3 rows per connection)
- [ ] Lopmon spawns coding agent
